data.title: As a developer, an end-to-end acceptance test verifies the full ticket delete flow across all subsystems
data.description:
Story: Write an end-to-end acceptance test that exercises the complete delete lifecycle -- from CLI invocation through event sourcing, status compilation, transition blocking, parent closure, and bridge dispatch -- using mocked external dependencies.

Done Definitions:
- Acceptance test creates a fixture epic with two child stories, transitions one to closed, then deletes the other via ticket delete --user-approved
- Test asserts: (a) deleted ticket has status==deleted and archived==True in compiled state, (b) parent epic can be closed after one child is closed and one is deleted (CLOSED_STATUSES gate passes), (c) ticket list output excludes the deleted ticket by default, (d) ticket transition <deleted-id> <current> closed exits non-zero with the instructional error, (e) any blocker dependency where the deleted ticket was the blocker has been removed
- Bridge mock receives a delete call for the deleted ticket's Jira key and does not receive a status-transition call
- Test is runnable in isolation: bash tests/acceptance/test-ticket-delete-e2e.sh
- Test is registered in .test-index

Considerations:
- [Dependency] Requires S1 (ticket-delete.sh), S2 (transition guard + CLOSED_STATUSES), and S3 (bridge mock interface) to be complete before this test can be written meaningfully
- [Testing] Use the existing test fixture patterns from tests/acceptance/ -- do not introduce new fixture infrastructure
- [Scope] This story tests the integration of all delete-related SCs; individual unit tests live in S1, S2, and S3
